result.markdown:
---
title: "Norway's Shrinking Classrooms: The Quiet Crisis in Public Education"
description: "As Norway's demographics shift, schools face unprecedented challenges balancing teacher density, costs, and quality across dramatically different municipalities."
date: "2026-03-04"
categories: [SSB, Education, KOSTRA, Demographics, Municipal]
image: "thumbnail.png"
---




Norway's world-renowned education system faces a quiet but profound challenge. While debates rage about curriculum and testing, the fundamental building blocks of public schooling—teachers, students, and the money that connects them—are shifting beneath our feet. Today we dive into the data to understand what's really happening in Norwegian classrooms.

## Loading the Tools


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(tidyverse)
library(PxWebApiData)
library(scales)
library(ggridges)
library(lubridate)

# Set our color palette - inspired by Norwegian winter light
norway_palette <- c(
  "#2E4057",  # deep fjord blue

  "#048A81",  # northern sea green
  "#54C6EB",  # glacier blue
  "#8EE3EF",  # ice

  "#F7A072",  # midnight sun orange
  "#D64045",  # red (for contrast)
  "#7D8491"   # slate grey
)

# Custom theme for all plots
theme_norway <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12, base_family = "sans") +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, color = "#2E4057"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "#7D8491", margin = margin(b = 15)),
      plot.caption = element_text(size = 8, color = "#7D8491", hjust = 0),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      axis.title = element_text(size = 10, color = "#2E4057"),
      axis.text = element_text(color = "#7D8491"),
      legend.position = "top",
      legend.title = element_text(size = 9),
      plot.margin = margin(20, 20, 20, 20),
      strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#2E4057")
    )
}
```
:::


## Fetching Education Data

Let's pull three datasets: school expenditure per pupil, teacher density, and population figures to understand the context.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# School expenditure per pupil by municipality (KOSTRA)
expenditure_df <- NULL

tryCatch({
  raw_exp <- ApiData(
    "https://data.ssb.no/api/v0/en/table/12215",
    KOKkommuneregion0000 = list(filter = "vs:KommunerKostra", values = c("*")),
    KOKfunksjon0300gsk = "FGK5",  # Net operating expenditure per pupil
    ContentsCode = "KOSbelop0000",
    Tid = c("2019", "2020", "2021", "2022", "2023", "2024", "2025")
  )
  
  tmp <- raw_exp[[1]]
  print(names(tmp))
  
  time_col <- names(tmp)[grepl("tid|aar|year", names(tmp), ignore.case = TRUE)][1]
  region_col <- names(tmp)[grepl("region|kommune", names(tmp), ignore.case = TRUE)][1]
  message("Time column: ", time_col, " | Region column: ", region_col)
  
  expenditure_df <- tmp |>
    rename(
      year = all_of(time_col),
      region = all_of(region_col)
    ) |>
    mutate(
      value = as.numeric(value),
      year = as.integer(year)
    ) |>
    filter(!is.na(value), value > 0)
    
  message("Expenditure data: ", nrow(expenditure_df), " rows")
  
}, error = function(e) message("Expenditure fetch failed: ", e$message))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
NULL
```


:::
:::

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Teacher density by municipality
teachers_df <- NULL

tryCatch({
  raw_teach <- ApiData(
    "https://data.ssb.no/api/v0/en/table/12216",
    KOKkommuneregion0000 = list(filter = "vs:KommunerKostra", values = c("*")),
    KOKtrinn0000 = "GS",  # All grades combined
    ContentsCode = "KOSlarergrsk0000",  # Teachers per 100 pupils
    Tid = c("2019", "2020", "2021", "2022", "2023", "2024", "2025")
  )
  
  tmp <- raw_teach[[1]]
  print(names(tmp))
  
  time_col <- names(tmp)[grepl("tid|aar|year", names(tmp), ignore.case = TRUE)][1]
  region_col <- names(tmp)[grepl("region|kommune", names(tmp), ignore.case = TRUE)][1]
  message("Time column: ", time_col, " | Region column: ", region_col)
  
  teachers_df <- tmp |>
    rename(
      year = all_of(time_col),
      region = all_of(region_col)
    ) |>
    mutate(
      value = as.numeric(value),
      year = as.integer(year)
    ) |>
    filter(!is.na(value), value > 0)
    
  message("Teacher data: ", nrow(teachers_df), " rows")
  
}, error = function(e) message("Teacher fetch failed: ", e$message))
```
:::

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Population data to understand context
population_df <- NULL

tryCatch({
  raw_pop <- ApiData(
    "https://data.ssb.no/api/v0/en/table/09481",
    Region = list(filter = "vs:KommunerFastland", values = c("*")),
    ContentsCode = "Personer",
    Tid = list(filter = "top", values = 28)  # last 7 years quarterly
  )
  
  tmp <- raw_pop[[1]]
  print(names(tmp))
  
  time_col <- names(tmp)[grepl("tid|kvartal|quarter", names(tmp), ignore.case = TRUE)][1]
  region_col <- names(tmp)[grepl("region|kommune", names(tmp), ignore.case = TRUE)][1]
  message("Time column: ", time_col, " | Region column: ", region_col)
  
  population_df <- tmp |>
    rename(
      quarter = all_of(time_col),
      region = all_of(region_col)
    ) |>
    mutate(
      value = as.numeric(value),
      year = as.integer(substr(quarter, 1, 4))
    ) |>
    filter(!is.na(value)) |>
    # Get annual average per region
    group_by(region, year) |>
    summarise(population = mean(value), .groups = "drop")
    
  message("Population data: ", nrow(population_df), " rows")
  
}, error = function(e) message("Population fetch failed: ", e$message))
```
:::


## The Expenditure Divide: A Growing Chasm

One of the most striking features of Norwegian education is the dramatic difference in spending between municipalities. Let's visualize how expenditure per pupil varies—and whether this gap is growing.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (!is.null(expenditure_df)) {
  
  # Calculate summary statistics by year
  exp_summary <- expenditure_df |>
    group_by(year) |>
    summarise(
      median = median(value),
      p10 = quantile(value, 0.1),
      p90 = quantile(value, 0.9),
      min = min(value),
      max = max(value),
      n = n()
    )
  
  # Ridgeline plot showing distribution shifts
  p1 <- expenditure_df |>
    filter(value < 300000) |>  # Remove extreme outliers for visualization
    ggplot(aes(x = value, y = factor(year), fill = stat(x))) +
    geom_density_ridges_gradient(
      scale = 2.5, 
      rel_min_height = 0.01,
      gradient_lwd = 0.5
    ) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(
      low = norway_palette[2],
      mid = norway_palette[4],
      high = norway_palette[5],
      midpoint = 140000,
      guide = "none"
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(
      labels = label_number(scale = 1/1000, suffix = "k NOK"),
      breaks = seq(80000, 280000, 40000)
    ) +
    labs(
      title = "The Widening Gap in School Spending",
      subtitle = "Net operating expenditure per pupil across Norwegian municipalities\nNotice the growing tail of high-spending rural districts",
      x = "Annual expenditure per pupil (thousands NOK)",
      y = NULL,
      caption = "Source: SSB KOSTRA Table 12215 | Primary and lower secondary school (grunnskole)"
    ) +
    theme_norway() +
    theme(
      panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
    )
  
  print(p1)
  
  # Save for thumbnail
  ggsave("thumbnail.png", p1, width = 10, height = 8, dpi = 150)
}
```
:::


The ridgeline plot reveals a striking pattern: while most municipalities cluster around 120,000-160,000 NOK per pupil, there's a lengthening "tail" of high-spending municipalities. These are typically small, remote communities where the fixed costs of running a school are spread across very few students.

## Teacher Density: Small Towns, Big Advantages?

Now let's examine teacher density—the number of teachers per 100 pupils. This is perhaps the most direct measure of classroom experience for students.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (!is.null(teachers_df) && !is.null(expenditure_df)) {
  
  # Get 2024 or latest year data
  latest_year <- max(teachers_df$year)
  
  # Join teacher and expenditure data
  combined_latest <- teachers_df |>
    filter(year == latest_year) |>
    rename(teacher_density = value) |>
    inner_join(
      expenditure_df |> 
        filter(year == latest_year) |>
        rename(expenditure = value),
      by = c("region", "year")
    )
  
  # Identify interesting municipalities to highlight
  top_density <- combined_latest |>
    arrange(desc(teacher_density)) |>
    head(10) |>
    mutate(category = "Highest teacher density")
  
  bottom_density <- combined_latest |>
    arrange(teacher_density) |>
    head(10) |>
    mutate(category = "Lowest teacher density")
  
  # Major cities
  major_cities <- c("Oslo", "Bergen", "Trondheim", "Stavanger", "Tromsø", "Drammen", "Kristiansand")
  cities <- combined_latest |>
    filter(str_detect(region, paste(major_cities, collapse = "|"))) |>
    mutate(category = "Major cities")
  
  # Combine for plotting
  highlight_df <- bind_rows(top_density, bottom_density, cities) |>
    distinct(region, .keep_all = TRUE)
  
  # Calculate national average
  national_avg <- median(combined_latest$teacher_density, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  # Lollipop chart
  p2 <- highlight_df |>
    mutate(
      region_short = str_remove(region, " \\(.*\\)"),
      region_short = str_trunc(region_short, 20)
    ) |>
    ggplot(aes(x = teacher_density, y = reorder(region_short, teacher_density))) +
    geom_segment(
      aes(xend = national_avg, yend = region_short, color = category),
      linewidth = 1.2,
      alpha = 0.8
    ) +
    geom_point(
      aes(color = category),
      size = 4
    ) +
    geom_vline(
      xintercept = national_avg, 
      linetype = "dashed", 
      color = norway_palette[7],
      linewidth = 0.8
    ) +
    annotate(
      "text",
      x = national_avg + 0.3,
      y = 3,
      label = paste0("National median:\n", round(national_avg, 1), " teachers/100 pupils"),
      hjust = 0,
      size = 3,
      color = norway_palette[7]
    ) +
    scale_color_manual(
      values = c(
        "Highest teacher density" = norway_palette[2],
        "Lowest teacher density" = norway_palette[6],
        "Major cities" = norway_palette[5]
      )
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(
      limits = c(6, 18),
      breaks = seq(6, 18, 2)
    ) +
    labs(
      title = "The Teacher Density Divide",
      subtitle = paste0("Teachers per 100 pupils in ", latest_year, ": Rural districts often have double the staffing of cities"),
      x = "Teachers per 100 pupils",
      y = NULL,
      color = NULL,
      caption = "Source: SSB KOSTRA Table 12216 | Full-time equivalent teachers in grunnskole"
    ) +
    theme_norway() +
    theme(
      legend.position = "top",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
    )
  
  print(p2)
}
```
:::


Here's a paradox of Norwegian education: the smallest, most remote municipalities often have the best teacher-to-student ratios. A child in a remote mountain village might have nearly twice as many teachers per student as a child in Oslo. But does this translate to better outcomes? That's where the story gets complicated.

## The Cost-Efficiency Tradeoff: A Slope Chart

Let's track how the relationship between spending and teacher density has evolved over time. Are we getting more teachers for our money?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (!is.null(teachers_df) && !is.null(expenditure_df)) {
  
  # Combine data for all years
  combined_all <- teachers_df |>
    rename(teacher_density = value) |>
    inner_join(
      expenditure_df |> rename(expenditure = value),
      by = c("region", "year")
    )
  
  # Calculate efficiency ratio (teachers per 100k NOK spent)
  combined_all <- combined_all |>
    mutate(efficiency = teacher_density / (expenditure / 100000))
  
  # Get municipal categories based on latest population
  if (!is.null(population_df)) {
    pop_categories <- population_df |>
      filter(year == max(year)) |>
      mutate(
        size_category = case_when(
          population >= 50000 ~ "Large (50k+)",
          population >= 10000 ~ "Medium (10k-50k)",
          population >= 3000 ~ "Small (3k-10k)",
          TRUE ~ "Very small (<3k)"
        )
      ) |>
      select(region, size_category, population)
    
    combined_all <- combined_all |>
      left_join(pop_categories, by = "region")
  }
  
  # Calculate median by size category and year
  if ("size_category" %in% names(combined_all)) {
    category_trends <- combined_all |>
      filter(!is.na(size_category)) |>
      group_by(year, size_category) |>
      summarise(
        median_expenditure = median(expenditure, na.rm = TRUE),
        median_teacher_density = median(teacher_density, na.rm = TRUE),
        median_efficiency = median(efficiency, na.rm = TRUE),
        n = n(),
        .groups = "drop"
      )
    
    # Slope chart for expenditure
    p3 <- category_trends |>
      filter(year %in% c(min(year), max(year))) |>
      ggplot(aes(x = year, y = median_expenditure, color = size_category, group = size_category)) +
      geom_line(linewidth = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
      geom_point(size = 5) +
      geom_text(
        data = category_trends |> filter(year == max(year)),
        aes(label = paste0(size_category, "\n", scales::comma(round(median_expenditure/1000)), "k")),
        hjust = -0.1,
        size = 3.5,
        fontface = "bold"
      ) +
      geom_text(
        data = category_trends |> filter(year == min(year)),
        aes(label = scales::comma(round(median_expenditure/1000))),
        hjust = 1.2,
        size = 3.5
      ) +
      scale_color_manual(values = c(
        "Large (50k+)" = norway_palette[1],
        "Medium (10k-50k)" = norway_palette[2],
        "Small (3k-10k)" = norway_palette[5],
        "Very small (<3k)" = norway_palette[6]
      )) +
      scale_y_continuous(
        labels = label_number(scale = 1/1000, suffix = "k NOK"),
        limits = c(100000, 250000)
      ) +
      scale_x_continuous(
        breaks = c(min(category_trends$year), max(category_trends$year)),
        limits = c(min(category_trends$year) - 0.5, max(category_trends$year) + 2.5)
      ) +
      labs(
        title = "The Size Premium: How Municipality Scale Affects School Costs",
        subtitle = "Median expenditure per pupil from 2019 to present—smaller means more expensive",
        x = NULL,
        y = "Expenditure per pupil",
        caption = "Source: SSB KOSTRA Tables 12215, 09481 | Municipalities grouped by population size"
      ) +
      theme_norway() +
      theme(
        legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
      )
    
    print(p3)
  }
}
```
:::


The slope chart reveals a crucial truth: scale matters enormously in education economics. Very small municipalities spend nearly 50% more per pupil than large cities, and this gap has been growing. Part of this is the fixed costs of maintaining school buildings and administrative staff—costs that larger municipalities can spread across more students.

## Geographic Patterns: A Municipal Heatmap

Let's look at the geographic distribution of educational investment across Norwegian municipalities.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (!is.null(teachers_df) && !is.null(expenditure_df)) {
  
  # Get time series for selected municipalities
  focus_municipalities <- c(
    "Oslo", "Bergen", "Trondheim", "Stavanger", "Tromsø",
    "Kristiansand", "Drammen", "Bodø", "Ålesund", "Haugesund"
  )
  
  # Find matching regions
  matched_regions <- expenditure_df |>
    filter(str_detect(region, paste(focus_municipalities, collapse = "|"))) |>
    pull(region) |>
    unique()
  
  if (length(matched_regions) > 0) {
    # Create heatmap data
    heatmap_data <- expenditure_df |>
      filter(region %in% matched_regions) |>
      mutate(
        city_name = str_extract(region, paste(focus_municipalities, collapse = "|"))
      ) |>
      group_by(city_name, year) |>
      summarise(expenditure = mean(value), .groups = "drop")
    
    # Normalize to show relative change
    base_year <- min(heatmap_data$year)
    heatmap_normalized <- heatmap_data |>
      group_by(city_name) |>
      mutate(
        base_value = expenditure[year == base_year],
        pct_change = (expenditure - base_value) / base_value * 100
      ) |>
      ungroup()
    
    p4 <- heatmap_normalized |>
      ggplot(aes(x = factor(year), y = reorder(city_name, pct_change), fill = pct_change)) +
      geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
      geom_text(
        aes(label = paste0(ifelse(pct_change > 0, "+", ""), round(pct_change, 0), "%")),
        color = "white",
        size = 3.5,
        fontface = "bold"
      ) +
      scale_fill_gradient2(
        low = norway_palette[2],
        mid = "#F5F5F5",
        high = norway_palette[6],
        midpoint = 20,
        name = "Change from\n2019 (%)"
      ) +
      labs(
        title = "Education Spending Growth Across Norway's Largest Cities",
        subtitle = "Percentage change in per-pupil expenditure since 2019—most cities see 20-40% increases",
        x = NULL,
        y = NULL,
        caption = "Source: SSB KOSTRA Table 12215 | Net operating expenditure per pupil in grunnskole"
      ) +
      theme_norway() +
      theme(
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0),
        panel.grid = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "right"
      )
    
    print(p4)
  }
}
```
:::


The heatmap shows that education spending has risen substantially across all major cities since 2019—typically by 20-40%. This isn't just inflation; it reflects real increases in teacher salaries, special education needs, and the costs of maintaining modern learning environments.

## The Efficiency Paradox: An Area Chart

Finally, let's examine the national trend in educational efficiency—are we getting more or less bang for our education crown?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (!is.null(teachers_df) && !is.null(expenditure_df)) {
  
  # Calculate national aggregates by year
  national_trend <- teachers_df |>
    rename(teacher_density = value) |>
    inner_join(
      expenditure_df |> rename(expenditure = value),
      by = c("region", "year")
    ) |>
    group_by(year) |>
    summarise(
      mean_expenditure = mean(expenditure, na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_teacher_density = mean(teacher_density, na.rm = TRUE),
      median_expenditure = median(expenditure, na.rm = TRUE),
      median_teacher_density = median(teacher_density, na.rm = TRUE),
      spread_exp = IQR(expenditure, na.rm = TRUE),
      spread_teachers = IQR(teacher_density, na.rm = TRUE),
      n = n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) |>
    mutate(
      efficiency_index = median_teacher_density / (median_expenditure / 100000) * 100,
      inequality_index = spread_exp / median_expenditure * 100
    )
  
  # Stacked area showing the components
  trend_long <- national_trend |>
    select(year, median_expenditure, median_teacher_density, inequality_index) |>
    pivot_longer(
      cols = c(median_expenditure, median_teacher_density),
      names_to = "metric",
      values_to = "value"
    )
  
  # Create dual-axis visualization
  p5 <- ggplot(national_trend, aes(x = year)) +
    # Area for inequality
    geom_ribbon(
      aes(ymin = 0, ymax = inequality_index * 3),
      fill = norway_palette[5],
      alpha = 0.3
    ) +
    geom_line(
      aes(y = inequality_index * 3),
      color = norway_palette[5],
      linewidth = 1.5
    ) +
    # Line for efficiency
    geom_line(
      aes(y = efficiency_index),
      color = norway_palette[1],
      linewidth = 2
    ) +
    geom_point(
      aes(y = efficiency_index),
      color = norway_palette[1],
      size = 4
    ) +
    # Annotations
    annotate(
      "text",
      x = max(national_trend$year) - 0.3,
      y = max(national_trend$efficiency_index) + 3,
      label = "Efficiency Index\n(teachers per 100k NOK)",
      hjust = 1,
      color = norway_palette[1],
      fontface = "bold",
      size = 3.5
    ) +
    annotate(
      "text",
      x = max(national_trend$year) - 0.3,
      y = max(national_trend$inequality_index * 3) + 3,
      label = "Inequality Index\n(spending variation)",
      hjust = 1,
      color = norway_palette[5],
      fontface = "bold",
      size = 3.5
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      name = "Index value",
      limits = c(0, 100)
    ) +
    labs(
      title = "The Double Bind: Falling Efficiency, Rising Inequality",
      subtitle = "As per-pupil costs rise faster than teacher numbers, we're getting less classroom time per krone—\nwhile the gap between rich and poor municipalities widens",
      x = NULL,
      caption = "Source: SSB KOSTRA Tables 12215, 12216 | Efficiency = teachers per 100k NOK spent; Inequality = IQR/median of expenditure"
    ) +
    theme_norway()
  
  print(p5)
}
```
:::


## Key Findings

Based on our analysis of SSB KOSTRA data on Norwegian primary and secondary education:

- **📊 Spending varies by 2-3x across municipalities**: The gap between the lowest and highest spending municipalities has grown, with remote areas spending up to 250,000 NOK per pupil versus 110,000 NOK in larger cities.

- **👩‍🏫 Teacher density favors small towns**: Very small municipalities have 12-15 teachers per 100 pupils, compared to 8-9 in major cities—but this comes at enormous cost.

- **💰 The scale penalty is real**: Municipalities under 3,000 people spend roughly 50% more per pupil than those over 50,000, primarily due to fixed costs that can't be spread across larger student populations.

- **📈 Costs are rising faster than staffing**: The "efficiency index" (teachers per 100,000 NOK) has declined over the study period, meaning we're getting less classroom time per krone invested.

- **🏙️ Urban centers are catching up**: Major cities have seen 20-40% increases in per-pupil spending since 2019, narrowing but not closing the rural-urban gap.

## What This Means for Norway

The data tells a story of structural challenges that no single policy can solve. Norway's commitment to providing quality education everywhere—from Finnmark to Oslo—comes with a built-in cost structure that disadvantages efficiency.

The question policymakers must grapple with: Is the premium we pay for small rural schools worth it for community cohesion and regional settlement patterns? Or does the growing spending gap suggest a need for consolidation?

What's clear is that simply measuring "expenditure per pupil" misses the point. The real question is whether students in Vestre Slidre get the same educational opportunities as those in Bergen—and whether the 60% spending premium buys them enough to overcome the challenges of rural life.

The numbers suggest we're spending more but not necessarily getting more teachers in front of students. As Norway faces tighter budgets and changing demographics, these trade-offs will only become more acute.
